system_context.business_goals: Provide a fully automated test environment for Prolog programs.

architectures.name: PROTest II - Automated Prolog Testing Environment
architectures.description: A pipeline-based testing architecture that processes Prolog programs through sequential stages of analysis, test generation, execution, and reporting. The system follows a linear workflow where each component performs a specialized testing function, passing results to downstream components. Components operate as a cohesive unit within a single testing environment.
architectures.architecture_characteristics: Sequential processing with clear separation of concerns. Moderate modularity with components organized by testing phase. Limited scalability due to linear pipeline design. Strong cohesion within testing workflow. Designed for single-user, interactive testing sessions rather than concurrent or distributed testing.

key_components.responsibilities: Entry point for testing workflow. Analyzes Prolog source code structure to validate syntax and identify testable predicates/clauses. Provides structural metadata to downstream components about code organization and complexity.
key_components.change_sensitivity: Medium - Changes affect what code can be tested and how test inputs are generated. Modifications here impact the entire downstream pipeline.

key_components.responsibilities: Automatically creates test inputs based on coverage criteria and structural analysis. Uses information from Structure Checker to generate inputs targeting specific clauses and execution paths. Determines which test cases will be executed.
key_components.change_sensitivity: High - Central to automation goal. Changes to generation algorithms affect test quality, coverage achievement, and automation level. Depends on both structural analysis and coverage feedback, making it a critical integration point.

key_components.responsibilities: Executes generated test cases against the Prolog program under test. Manages test execution environment, captures outputs, and collects execution traces for coverage analysis. Acts as the runtime engine for the testing process.
key_components.change_sensitivity: Medium - Changes affect execution capabilities (e.g., timeout handling, parallel execution, debugging support). Modifications impact what types of tests can be run but not test generation or analysis logic.

key_components.responsibilities: Instruments and analyzes Prolog source code to measure clause coverage. Tracks which clauses were executed during testing, identifies untested code paths, and provides feedback to Test Input Generator for iterative coverage improvement.
key_components.change_sensitivity: High - Defines what 'adequate testing' means. Changes to coverage metrics (e.g., adding predicate coverage, path coverage, or data flow coverage) significantly impact the entire testing strategy and automation effectiveness.

key_components.responsibilities: Synthesizes results from all components into comprehensive test reports. Presents coverage metrics, test outcomes, structural analysis findings, and identifies gaps in testing. Provides actionable feedback to users.
key_components.change_sensitivity: Low - Primarily presentation layer. Changes affect how information is displayed but not core testing logic. Can be modified independently to support different report formats or visualizations.

architectures.data_flow: Linear pipeline with feedback loop: (1) Structure Checker analyzes source code and extracts structural metadata → (2) Test Input Generator uses structural information and coverage goals to create test cases → (3) Test Driver executes tests against instrumented code → (4) Test Coverage Analyzer measures clause coverage from execution traces and feeds back to Test Input Generator for iterative improvement → (5) Test Report Generator aggregates data from all components to produce final reports. The feedback loop between Coverage Analyzer and Input Generator enables progressive coverage improvement across multiple iterations.
architectural_constraints: Sequential pipeline design limits parallel test execution and concurrent user sessions
Tight coupling between Test Input Generator and Test Coverage Analyzer creates dependency on clause coverage as the primary metric
Single-environment design assumes one program under test at a time
Prolog-specific implementation limits portability to other logic programming languages
Interactive nature assumes human-in-the-loop for test validation and interpretation
Automation is 'partial' - requires manual intervention for test oracle definition and result validation
architectures.notes:
WHAT THIS ARCHITECTURE SUPPORTS WELL: (1) Automated clause coverage testing for Prolog programs, (2) Iterative test improvement through coverage feedback, (3) Integrated workflow from analysis to reporting, (4) Single-user interactive testing sessions, (5) Structural analysis-driven test generation. 

WHAT THIS ARCHITECTURE STRUGGLES WITH: (1) Fully automated testing (still requires manual oracle definition - gap from business goal), (2) Concurrent testing of multiple programs, (3) Distributed or cloud-based testing, (4) Alternative coverage metrics beyond clause coverage, (5) Integration with CI/CD pipelines, (6) Performance testing or large-scale test suites, (7) Test case reuse across projects. 

CHANGE IMPACT EXAMPLES: Adding predicate-level coverage would require changes to C-04 (high effort) and C-02 (high effort). Supporting parallel test execution would require architectural changes to C-03 and potentially C-04 (very high effort). Adding new report formats affects only C-05 (low effort). Supporting multiple Prolog dialects would impact C-01, C-03, and C-04 (high effort). Achieving 'fully automated' testing (per business goal) would require significant enhancement to C-02 and potentially a new oracle generation component (very high effort).

scenario_elicitation.elicitation_method: AI-Driven Brainstorming based on System Context

scenarios.scenario_text: A Prolog developer loads a program module containing 50 predicates and initiates automated testing. The system automatically generates test inputs for all predicates, executes tests without manual intervention, measures code coverage, and produces a comprehensive test report within 5 minutes.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Requires enhancement to current 'partially automated' system. All pipeline components exist but need full automation capabilities. Current architecture supports sequential processing but may require intelligent test input generation algorithms and autonomous decision-making capabilities not currently specified.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Developers need to reduce time spent on manual test creation and execution. Full automation eliminates repetitive tasks and allows focus on development rather than testing mechanics.

scenarios.scenario_text: An end-user interactively reviews test results for a Prolog program, selects 3 failing test cases from the Test Report Generator output, manually adjusts test inputs through the interface, and re-executes only those specific tests to verify fixes.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Directly supported by current 'interactive' and 'partially automated' architecture. The system description explicitly mentions interactive capabilities and partial automation, suggesting manual intervention points exist in the workflow.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: End-users need control over the testing process to investigate specific failures, understand test behavior, and validate corrections iteratively without running entire test suites.

scenarios.scenario_text: A system administrator monitors the Test Driver component processing 200 concurrent test executions across 10 different Prolog programs, observes resource utilization metrics (CPU, memory), and receives alerts when any test execution exceeds 2-minute timeout threshold.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Not directly supported by current architecture. The pipeline-based, linear workflow suggests sequential rather than concurrent processing. Would require architectural changes to support parallel execution, resource monitoring infrastructure, and alerting mechanisms not mentioned in current design.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: System administrators need visibility into system performance and resource consumption to ensure reliable operation, prevent system overload, and maintain service level agreements in multi-user environments.

scenarios.scenario_text: A maintainer updates the Test Coverage Analyzer component to support a new coverage metric (predicate call chains). The change is isolated to this component, requires no modifications to upstream (Test Driver) or downstream (Test Report Generator) components, and is deployed without system downtime.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Partially supported by current architecture. The pipeline design with specialized components suggests some modularity, but 'cohesive unit within a single testing environment' indicates tight coupling. Component interfaces and data contracts are not specified, making true isolation uncertain. May require interface standardization.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Maintainers need to evolve system capabilities without cascading changes across components. Modular architecture reduces maintenance costs, testing burden, and deployment risks when adding features or fixing defects.

scenarios.scenario_text: A developer integrates PROTest II into their continuous integration pipeline, where each code commit triggers automated structure checking and test generation. The system processes 15 commits per day, automatically generates regression tests for modified predicates, and provides pass/fail feedback within 3 minutes per commit.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Requires significant architectural enhancement. Current 'interactive' design suggests manual initiation rather than event-driven automation. Would need API interfaces, webhook support, version control integration, and automated triggering mechanisms not present in current pipeline architecture.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Modern development workflows require automated testing integrated into CI/CD pipelines. Developers need immediate feedback on code changes to maintain code quality and prevent regression defects from reaching production.

scenarios.scenario_text: An end-user runs the Structure Checker on a 1000-line Prolog program containing syntax errors and logical inconsistencies. The system identifies 12 structural issues, provides specific line numbers and error descriptions, and allows the user to correct errors and re-check without restarting the entire testing workflow.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Directly supported by current architecture. Structure Checker is explicitly listed as a key component in the pipeline. The interactive nature of the system supports iterative checking and correction workflows without full pipeline re-execution.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: End-users need early detection of structural problems before investing time in test development. Interactive error correction reduces frustration and accelerates the development-test cycle by catching issues at the earliest pipeline stage.

scenarios.scenario_text: A maintainer diagnoses why test coverage reports are incomplete for recursive predicates. They enable detailed logging in the Test Coverage Analyzer, trace execution through 5 recursive predicate calls, identify the coverage calculation bug, and verify the fix using component-level unit tests.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Partially supported. While the Test Coverage Analyzer exists as a component, the architecture description doesn't specify observability features like logging, tracing, or component-level testing capabilities. May require adding diagnostic instrumentation to the existing component.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Maintainers need diagnostic capabilities to troubleshoot defects efficiently. Without visibility into component internals and execution traces, debugging becomes time-consuming and error-prone, increasing mean time to resolution.

scenarios.scenario_text: A system administrator configures the Test Input Generator to limit automatic test case generation to maximum 100 test cases per predicate to prevent resource exhaustion. The configuration persists across system restarts and applies uniformly to all users without requiring code changes.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Not directly supported. Current architecture doesn't mention configuration management, persistence mechanisms, or administrative controls. Would require adding configuration subsystem, storage for settings, and runtime enforcement mechanisms to the existing pipeline.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: System administrators need operational controls to prevent resource abuse and ensure fair resource allocation in shared environments. Configurable limits protect system stability and enable capacity planning.

scenario_classification.rationale: While all required pipeline components exist (C-01 through C-05), the architecture explicitly states automation is 'partial' and requires 'manual intervention for test oracle definition and result validation'. The notes section identifies 'Fully automated testing' as something the architecture 'STRUGGLES WITH' and achieving it would require 'very high effort'. The 5-minute performance target for 50 predicates also lacks validation against current sequential pipeline performance characteristics.

The architecture explicitly supports 'interactive testing sessions' and 'partial automation' with 'human-in-the-loop for test validation'. The feasibility note confirms this is 'Directly supported by current interactive and partially automated architecture'. The pipeline allows selective re-execution without full workflow restart, and C-02 (Test Input Generator), C-03 (Test Driver), and C-05 (Test Report Generator) provide the necessary capabilities for manual test adjustment and selective re-execution.

The architecture explicitly states 'Sequential pipeline design limits parallel test execution and concurrent user sessions' and 'Single-environment design assumes one program under test at a time'. The architectural constraints and characteristics describe a 'linear workflow' and 'single-user, interactive testing sessions rather than concurrent or distributed testing'. Supporting 200 concurrent executions across 10 programs would require fundamental architectural redesign from sequential to parallel/distributed processing, plus new monitoring and alerting infrastructure not present in current design.

While the architecture shows 'moderate modularity with components organized by testing phase', the notes explicitly state that 'Adding predicate-level coverage would require changes to C-04 (high effort) and C-02 (high effort)'. The architecture describes components as a 'cohesive unit within a single testing environment', suggesting tight coupling. C-04 has 'High' change sensitivity and is tightly coupled to C-02 through the feedback loop. The scenario's requirement for zero-downtime deployment and complete isolation is not supported by the current monolithic, single-environment design.

The architecture notes explicitly identify 'Integration with CI/CD pipelines' as something the architecture 'STRUGGLES WITH'. The current design is 'interactive' with 'human-in-the-loop', not event-driven or API-based. The sequential pipeline lacks webhook support, version control integration, automated triggering mechanisms, and API interfaces needed for CI/CD integration. The 'single-user, interactive testing sessions' characteristic contradicts the automated, event-driven nature required for CI/CD workflows.

C-01 (Structure Checker) is explicitly described as the 'Entry point for testing workflow' that 'Analyzes Prolog source code structure to validate syntax and identify testable predicates/clauses'. The interactive nature of the architecture supports 'iterative checking and correction workflows without full pipeline re-execution'. The Structure Checker has no dependencies, allowing it to operate independently for syntax validation before engaging the full pipeline. The scenario aligns perfectly with the component's stated responsibilities.

While C-04 (Test Coverage Analyzer) exists and is responsible for coverage measurement, the architecture description does not specify observability features like detailed logging, execution tracing, or component-level testing capabilities. The architecture notes mention 'interactive nature' but don't detail diagnostic instrumentation. The scenario requires capabilities (detailed logging, trace execution, component-level unit tests) that are not explicitly part of the current architecture specification, though they could potentially be added to the existing component with moderate effort.

The architecture description contains no mention of configuration management, administrative controls, persistence mechanisms, or multi-user resource governance. The 'single-user, interactive testing sessions' characteristic suggests the system lacks the multi-user administrative infrastructure needed for system-wide configuration. The scenario requires a configuration subsystem, persistent storage, runtime enforcement, and administrative interfaces that are not part of the current pipeline-based architecture.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: C-02 (Test Input Generator) requires enhancement to generate test oracles automatically, not just inputs. Currently relies on manual oracle definition. C-03 (Test Driver) needs autonomous test validation capabilities to compare actual vs expected results without human intervention. C-04 (Test Coverage Analyzer) may need optimization to meet 5-minute performance target for 50 predicates. Additionally, the feedback loop between C-04 and C-02 needs to operate autonomously without manual decision points. All components must support fully automated decision-making about test adequacy and completion.
issues_identified: Test oracle generation is a research-level problem for logic programming - no clear algorithmic solution exists
Autonomous determination of 'adequate testing' requires sophisticated heuristics beyond current clause coverage
Performance target (5 minutes for 50 predicates) is unvalidated against current sequential pipeline capabilities
Feedback loop automation may require machine learning or constraint solving techniques not in current architecture
Risk of generating invalid or meaningless test oracles without human validation
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Implement semi-automated approach: (1) Generate test inputs automatically but flag cases requiring manual oracle definition, (2) Build oracle library from previous manual validations for reuse, (3) Use property-based testing where users define high-level properties instead of specific oracles, (4) Implement confidence scoring for auto-generated oracles with manual review queue for low-confidence cases, (5) Focus automation on regression testing where previous results serve as oracles.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: No changes required. Current architecture directly supports this use case.
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: N/A - scenario is directly supported by current architecture

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Fundamental architectural transformation required: (1) C-03 (Test Driver) must be redesigned from sequential to parallel execution engine supporting concurrent test runs with process isolation, (2) Add resource monitoring subsystem to track CPU, memory, and execution time across concurrent processes, (3) Implement alert manager for timeout detection and notification, (4) Add multi-tenancy support to handle 10 different programs simultaneously with isolated execution contexts, (5) C-04 (Test Coverage Analyzer) needs thread-safe instrumentation for concurrent coverage collection, (6) Add scheduling and queuing infrastructure to manage 200 concurrent executions, (7) Implement administrative dashboard for system monitoring.
issues_identified: Sequential pipeline architecture fundamentally incompatible with concurrent execution model
Prolog execution environments may not support safe concurrent execution without process isolation
Resource monitoring adds significant overhead that may impact test execution performance
Coverage instrumentation (C-04) may have race conditions when multiple tests execute simultaneously
Single-environment design assumption violated - requires complete architectural rethinking
Alert infrastructure and monitoring systems are entirely new capabilities not in current design
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Implement batch processing model: (1) Queue test requests and process sequentially with status tracking, (2) Deploy multiple independent PROTest II instances (one per program) with external orchestration, (3) Implement time-sliced execution where each program gets sequential processing windows, (4) Use external monitoring tools (OS-level) rather than building into architecture, (5) Partition workload across multiple machines rather than concurrent execution on single system.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: While the change appears localized to C-04, the tight coupling in current architecture creates cascading impacts: (1) C-04 (Test Coverage Analyzer) needs new instrumentation for predicate call chain tracking, (2) C-02 (Test Input Generator) must be updated because it depends on C-04 coverage feedback - new coverage metric changes what 'adequate coverage' means and how test generation targets are set, (3) C-05 (Test Report Generator) needs updates to display new call chain coverage metrics, (4) Define and implement standardized interfaces between components to prevent future coupling, (5) Add versioned data contracts for coverage metrics, (6) Implement plugin architecture for coverage analyzers to enable true isolation, (7) Add zero-downtime deployment infrastructure (not present in single-environment design).
issues_identified: Tight coupling between C-02 and C-04 through feedback loop prevents isolated changes
C-04 has 'High' change sensitivity - changes significantly impact testing strategy
No standardized interfaces or data contracts specified in current architecture
Single-environment design doesn't support zero-downtime deployment
Call chain coverage is computationally expensive - may impact performance of entire pipeline
Lack of plugin architecture means coverage metrics are hardcoded into components
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Phased deployment approach: (1) Add call chain coverage as optional metric alongside clause coverage initially, (2) Implement feature flags to enable/disable new metric without redeployment, (3) Use adapter pattern to translate new coverage format to existing interface for C-02, (4) Deploy during maintenance window rather than zero-downtime, (5) Create abstraction layer for coverage metrics before adding new types, (6) Implement backward-compatible data formats that support both old and new metrics.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Transform from interactive to event-driven architecture: (1) Add API gateway layer to expose pipeline components as REST/webhook endpoints, (2) Implement event handler to receive commit notifications from version control systems, (3) Add VCS integration module to fetch changed code and identify modified predicates, (4) C-01 (Structure Checker) needs API interface for programmatic invocation, (5) C-02 (Test Input Generator) requires delta-based test generation focusing on changed predicates, (6) C-03 (Test Driver) needs headless execution mode without interactive prompts, (7) C-05 (Test Report Generator) must produce machine-readable output (JSON/XML) for CI/CD consumption, (8) Add test result persistence and history tracking, (9) Implement regression test selection based on code changes, (10) Add authentication/authorization for CI/CD integration.
issues_identified: Interactive design fundamentally conflicts with automated CI/CD workflows
No API interfaces or programmatic access points in current architecture
Human-in-the-loop assumption prevents autonomous operation required for CI/CD
3-minute performance target per commit requires optimization of sequential pipeline
Regression test selection requires test-to-code traceability not in current design
15 commits/day suggests need for queuing and concurrent processing (conflicts with sequential design)
Version control integration is entirely new capability requiring external system integration
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Hybrid approach: (1) Create CI/CD wrapper script that invokes PROTest II command-line interface in batch mode, (2) Use file-based integration where CI/CD writes code to watched directory and polls for results, (3) Implement scheduled batch processing of accumulated commits rather than per-commit triggering, (4) Use existing test suites for CI/CD and reserve PROTest II for interactive development, (5) Deploy PROTest II as containerized service with simple HTTP interface for basic automation, (6) Generate test cases interactively but execute them automatically in CI/CD using external test runner.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: No changes required. Current architecture directly supports this use case.
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: N/A - scenario is directly supported by current architecture

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Add observability infrastructure to C-04 (Test Coverage Analyzer): (1) Implement configurable logging framework with multiple verbosity levels (ERROR, WARN, INFO, DEBUG, TRACE), (2) Add execution tracing capability to capture predicate call sequences and coverage calculation steps, (3) Implement component-level unit testing infrastructure with test harnesses for isolated C-04 testing, (4) Add instrumentation points for recursive predicate tracking specifically, (5) Create diagnostic output formats (trace files, debug logs) separate from production reports, (6) Implement log aggregation and filtering for recursive call analysis, (7) Add performance profiling to identify coverage calculation bottlenecks.
issues_identified: Current architecture lacks specified observability or diagnostic capabilities
No mention of component-level testing infrastructure in architecture description
Logging framework not specified - may need to add logging library dependency
Trace execution for recursive predicates may generate large volumes of diagnostic data
Performance impact of detailed logging on production testing workflows unclear
No standardized diagnostic output format across components
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Lightweight diagnostic approach: (1) Add debug mode flag to enable verbose output without full logging framework, (2) Use Prolog's built-in trace/debug predicates for execution tracing, (3) Implement post-mortem analysis by saving intermediate coverage data for offline inspection, (4) Create separate diagnostic version of C-04 with instrumentation rather than adding to production component, (5) Use external profiling tools (Prolog debugger) rather than building into component, (6) Add assertion-based validation that fails fast on coverage calculation errors.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Add configuration management infrastructure: (1) Implement configuration manager component to store and retrieve system settings, (2) Add persistence layer (file-based or database) for configuration storage across restarts, (3) Modify C-02 (Test Input Generator) to check configuration limits before generating test cases, (4) Implement runtime enforcement in C-02 to cap test generation at configured maximum, (5) Add administrative interface for configuration management (CLI or GUI), (6) Implement configuration validation to prevent invalid settings, (7) Add multi-user support to apply configurations uniformly across all users, (8) Implement configuration versioning and audit trail for administrative changes.
issues_identified: No configuration management subsystem in current architecture
Single-user design lacks multi-user infrastructure for uniform configuration application
No persistence mechanism specified for storing settings across restarts
Administrative controls and interfaces not part of current design
Configuration changes may require component restart in current monolithic design
No role-based access control for administrative vs. regular user functions
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Simplified configuration approach: (1) Use environment variables or configuration files read at startup, (2) Implement hard-coded limits in C-02 that require code changes to modify, (3) Add command-line parameters for test generation limits passed per execution, (4) Use external resource management (OS-level limits) rather than application-level configuration, (5) Document recommended limits in user guide rather than enforcing programmatically, (6) Implement per-session configuration that doesn't persist (user sets limits each time).

scenario_interaction_weights.rationale: C-02 (Test Input Generator) is the most critical hotspot affecting 4 high-impact scenarios. S-01 requires research-level oracle generation capabilities with high risk of invalid outputs. S-05 demands event-driven CI/CD integration transforming the interactive architecture - failure blocks modern DevOps adoption affecting all developers. S-04's tight coupling creates cascading changes preventing isolated evolution. S-08 adds administrative controls needed for multi-user environments. Not addressing this creates: (1) inability to achieve full automation blocking enterprise adoption, (2) CI/CD integration failure preventing modern workflow integration, (3) maintenance nightmares due to tight coupling, (4) resource exhaustion without administrative limits. All stakeholder roles blocked from critical use cases.

C-04 (Test Coverage Analyzer) is architectural linchpin with 'High' change sensitivity affecting 4 scenarios spanning automation, scalability, and maintainability. S-03 requires thread-safe concurrent coverage collection for 200 simultaneous executions - race conditions risk invalid coverage data corrupting all test results. S-04's tight coupling prevents adding new metrics without widespread changes. S-01 needs performance optimization for 5-minute target. S-07 lacks observability for debugging recursive predicate issues. Not addressing creates: (1) scalability ceiling blocking enterprise deployment (S-03), (2) inability to evolve coverage metrics as testing needs mature, (3) production debugging nightmares without diagnostic capabilities, (4) performance bottlenecks in automation workflows. Maintainers cannot debug, administrators cannot scale, developers cannot trust results.

C-03 (Test Driver) requires fundamental architectural transformation across 3 critical scenarios. S-03 demands redesign from sequential to parallel execution supporting 200 concurrent tests with process isolation - sequential architecture is existential blocker to enterprise scalability. S-05 requires headless execution mode for CI/CD automation affecting all development teams. S-01 needs autonomous validation without human intervention. Not addressing creates: (1) hard scalability limit preventing multi-user/multi-program deployment, (2) CI/CD integration failure blocking automated testing pipelines, (3) manual intervention requirements preventing full automation. System administrators cannot deploy at scale, developers cannot integrate with modern toolchains, end-users face performance bottlenecks. This is architectural debt that compounds over time.

C-05 (Test Report Generator) affects 2 scenarios but is output-facing rather than core logic. S-05 requires machine-readable formats (JSON/XML) for CI/CD consumption - currently human-readable reports block automated result processing. S-04 needs new visualizations for call chain coverage metrics. Not addressing creates: (1) manual result parsing in CI/CD pipelines reducing automation value, (2) incomplete coverage visibility limiting testing effectiveness, (3) integration friction with downstream tools. However, workarounds exist (parsing text reports, external visualization tools). Impact is primarily developer workflow efficiency and end-user insight quality rather than fundamental capability blocking. Lower priority than core execution/analysis components but still important for modern toolchain integration.

architectural_strengths: Strength 1: Strong support for core interactive testing workflows - S-02 and S-06 are directly supported without modifications, demonstrating the architecture successfully delivers on its primary design intent of interactive, single-user logic program testing. The sequential pipeline (C-01→C-02→C-03→C-04→C-05) effectively supports developers manually testing individual programs.

Strength 2: Clear component separation with defined responsibilities - Each component (Structure Checker, Test Input Generator, Test Driver, Coverage Analyzer, Report Generator) has a distinct, well-understood purpose. This conceptual clarity makes the system understandable and enables localized changes like S-07's observability enhancements to C-04, which required only 'Medium' effort with 'Low' risk.

Strength 3: Proven domain fit for logic programming testing - The architecture's focus on Prolog-specific concerns (clause coverage, predicate testing, structure checking) shows deep domain understanding. Components like C-04's coverage analysis are tailored to logic programming semantics, providing value that generic testing frameworks cannot deliver.
architectural_weaknesses: Weakness 1: Tight coupling prevents isolated evolution - The feedback loop between C-02 and C-04 creates cascading changes across scenarios. S-04's attempt to add call chain coverage to C-04 required 'Widespread' changes affecting C-02, C-05, and necessitating new interface standards. This coupling means no component can evolve independently, multiplying maintenance costs and deployment risks.

Weakness 2: Sequential, single-environment design fundamentally incompatible with enterprise scalability - S-03's concurrent execution requirement (200 simultaneous tests, 10 programs) exposes existential architectural limitations. The sequential pipeline cannot support multi-tenancy, parallel execution, or resource isolation. This creates a hard scalability ceiling blocking deployment beyond individual developers, affecting System Administrators and enterprise End-users.

Weakness 3: Interactive, human-in-the-loop assumptions block automation - S-01's full automation requirement and S-05's CI/CD integration both fail because the architecture assumes manual oracle definition, interactive prompts, and human decision-making. Three high-risk scenarios (S-01, S-03, S-05) all rated 'High' effort require transforming these fundamental assumptions, indicating the architecture is misaligned with modern automated DevOps workflows.

Weakness 4: Missing cross-cutting infrastructure for enterprise deployment - Configuration management (S-08), API interfaces (S-05), observability (S-07), event handling (S-05), and resource monitoring (S-03) are all absent. These aren't feature gaps but architectural omissions - the system lacks foundational layers needed for production deployment, multi-user environments, and operational management.

Weakness 5: No extensibility mechanisms for evolving requirements - S-04's new coverage metric and S-01's oracle generation both require invasive changes because the architecture hardcodes testing strategies rather than providing plugin points or abstraction layers. The lack of versioned data contracts, feature flags, or adapter patterns means every enhancement risks breaking existing functionality.
overall_results.modifiability_assessment: The architecture exhibits LOW modifiability - while conceptually clear, it is rigid and tightly coupled, requiring 'Widespread' changes for 5 of 8 scenarios. The sequential, interactive, single-user design creates fundamental incompatibilities with enterprise requirements (automation, scalability, CI/CD integration), necessitating architectural transformation rather than incremental evolution.

recommendations.recommendation: Decouple C-02 and C-04 through standardized coverage abstraction layer - Introduce versioned data contracts and event-based communication between Test Input Generator and Coverage Analyzer, eliminating direct feedback loop dependencies. Implement plugin architecture for coverage metrics allowing new analyzers (call chain, mutation, etc.) without modifying C-02.
recommendations.rationale: Addresses the #1 ranked hotspot (C-02) affecting 4 scenarios and #2 hotspot (C-04) affecting 4 scenarios. Breaks the tight coupling that causes 'Widespread' changes in S-04, enables independent evolution of testing strategies, and creates foundation for extensibility. This single refactoring reduces ripple effects across the most critical components, directly improving modifiability for 6 of 8 scenarios.

recommendations.recommendation: Transform C-03 Test Driver to support both interactive and headless execution modes with process isolation - Implement execution strategy pattern allowing sequential (current), batch, and parallel execution modes. Add process-level isolation for test runs and external orchestration interfaces. Maintain backward compatibility with interactive mode while enabling programmatic invocation.
recommendations.rationale: Addresses #3 ranked hotspot (C-03) blocking 3 critical scenarios including S-03 (enterprise scalability) and S-05 (CI/CD integration). This is the architectural pivot point between current single-user design and future enterprise deployment. Enables gradual migration: interactive users unaffected while CI/CD and multi-user scenarios become possible. Without this, the architecture has a hard scalability ceiling preventing market expansion.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement semi-automated testing approach with confidence-based oracle generation - Rather than attempting full automation (S-01's research-level problem), build hybrid system: auto-generate test inputs and oracles with confidence scoring, queue low-confidence cases for manual review, build reusable oracle library from validated cases, support property-based testing for user-defined invariants.
recommendations.rationale: Addresses S-01's 'High' risk automation requirement pragmatically. Full automation is infeasible (oracle generation is research-level problem), but semi-automation delivers 80% of value with manageable risk. Enables gradual improvement as oracle library grows, maintains quality through human validation of edge cases, and provides path to CI/CD integration (S-05) using high-confidence automated oracles for regression testing. Realistic approach that balances business goals with technical feasibility.

recommendations.recommendation: Add API gateway and event-driven integration layer for CI/CD workflows - Implement REST/webhook endpoints exposing pipeline components, add event handlers for VCS integration, create machine-readable output formats (JSON/XML) in C-05, implement headless execution mode in C-03. Use adapter pattern to wrap existing components rather than rewriting core logic.
recommendations.rationale: Addresses S-05's CI/CD integration (affecting all Developers) and C-05 hotspot. Enables modern DevOps workflows without requiring complete architectural rewrite. The adapter pattern allows incremental delivery: basic API integration first, then sophisticated event handling and regression test selection. This is the bridge between current interactive architecture and automated pipelines, unblocking developer productivity improvements.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement configuration management subsystem with persistence and administrative controls - Add configuration manager component, file/database persistence layer, runtime enforcement in C-02 for test generation limits, administrative interface (CLI initially, GUI later), multi-user configuration application, and audit trail for changes.
recommendations.rationale: Addresses S-08 and provides foundation for multi-user deployment. Prevents resource exhaustion issues that will emerge as usage scales. Relatively isolated change (doesn't require modifying core testing logic) but enables operational management needed for production deployment. Quick win that delivers immediate value to System Administrators while building infrastructure reusable for future scenarios (user preferences, team-level settings, etc.).

recommendations.recommendation: Add comprehensive observability infrastructure across all components - Implement structured logging framework with configurable verbosity, distributed tracing for pipeline execution, component-level unit testing harnesses, performance profiling instrumentation, and diagnostic output formats. Start with C-04 (S-07) then extend to other components.
recommendations.rationale: Addresses S-07's debugging requirements and creates foundation for operational excellence. Currently, production issues are black boxes - adding observability enables Maintainers to diagnose problems, optimize performance bottlenecks (critical for S-01's 5-minute target and S-05's 3-minute CI/CD target), and validate correctness of complex components like coverage analysis. This is technical debt that compounds over time - early investment prevents future crisis debugging.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement phased deployment infrastructure with feature flags and backward-compatible data formats - Add feature toggle framework, versioned data schemas supporting old and new formats simultaneously, blue-green deployment capability, and rollback mechanisms. Use this for S-04's call chain coverage rollout.
recommendations.rationale: Enables zero-downtime deployments and safe experimentation with new features. S-04's call chain coverage can be deployed as optional metric, validated in production, then promoted to default. Reduces risk of 'Widespread' changes by allowing gradual rollout and quick rollback. This is infrastructure investment that pays dividends across all future enhancements, transforming high-risk big-bang deployments into low-risk incremental releases.

recommendations.recommendation: Document architectural decision records (ADRs) and create explicit interface contracts - Capture rationale for current sequential design, interactive assumptions, and component coupling. Define formal interfaces between components with versioning strategy. Create architecture decision log for future changes.
recommendations.rationale: Prevents future architects from repeating analysis done here. The evaluations reveal implicit assumptions (single-user, interactive, sequential) that aren't documented - making them explicit helps future teams understand constraints and make informed tradeoffs. Low effort, high long-term value for Maintainers and future developers. This is foundational work that improves architectural understanding and decision quality.
